title: Update working memory from Telegram channel docs refresh
summary: Durable context learned during the historical session about Telegram allowlist documentation, shared channel access-control docs, Slack docs, and config-template drift.

claims.text: Manual Telegram channel configuration uses the `allowlist` field with string entries for numeric user IDs or usernames; `allowed_users` is not the Moltis manual config field, and DM access checks both the peer ID and username.

The channel overview documentation centralizes the shared access-control model for external channels: DM policy, group policy, mention mode, string allowlist matching, and OTP self-approval, while noting that group allowlist field names vary by channel.

The default config template's channel examples now show Telegram with `allowlist` strings plus access, group, OTP, reply, and streaming fields, and include a Slack bot section with Socket Mode, Events API, access-control, streaming, and thread-reply fields.

The channel documentation refresh was driven by OpenClaw-to-Moltis user feedback showing obsolete Telegram docs with `allowed_users = [123456789]`; future channel docs should be checked against the channel config/access source code when documenting fields, types, migration notes, or allowlist behavior.
Slack documentation examples should use obviously fake placeholder tokens rather than realistic Slack token-looking values that can trigger GitHub secret scanning.

sources.title: Claude Code session: Telegram bot config feedback

metadata.reason: Transcript lines 17-51 describe the user report from an OpenClaw migration and the obsolete Telegram example; lines 71-79 show the agent choosing to fix docs and template from source-code findings.
Transcript lines 160-164 record that GitHub secret scanning flagged Slack token examples and the session amended the docs to use clearly fake placeholders before pushing.
